does: This paper introduces a comprehensive evaluation framework that systematically assesses membership inference attacks across the full machine learning pipeline, including data characteristics, model architectures, training algorithms, and post-training modifications. The framework evaluates state-of-the-art MIA methods across diverse configurations using three complementary metrics: Balanced Accuracy for symmetric misclassification costs, TPR at low false positive rates, and additional measures to capture varying real-world deployment scenarios. By characterizing how different contextual factors affect MIA performance, the work provides practitioners with concrete guidance on when attacks succeed or fail, moving beyond benchmark results to understand privacy risks in practical settings.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on membership inference attacks that emerged as a fundamental privacy threat in machine learning, extending prior work that demonstrated how adversaries can determine if specific examples were in a model's training set. The paper responds to limitations in existing MIA evaluations, which typically rely on narrow benchmarks and fail to account for the heterogeneity of real-world deployment contexts. It connects to parallel work on privacy auditing, machine unlearning evaluation, and differential privacy research, which all depend on accurate privacy risk quantification. The framework synthesizes insights from prior work on MIA variants—including gradient-based, loss-based, and reference-model attacks—into a unified evaluation methodology.
enables: This framework enables more robust privacy auditing practices by allowing practitioners to assess MIA risks across their specific operational contexts before deployment. It supports the development of improved defenses by identifying which configurations are most vulnerable and why, facilitating targeted mitigation strategies. The work points toward better standardization in privacy evaluation across the ML community, similar to adversarial robustness benchmarking. Future research can build on this framework to develop context-aware privacy guarantees, improve unlearning verification methods, and design algorithms that maintain utility while reducing membership inference vulnerability across diverse real-world scenarios.
